text: Lecture	  29	  –	  Development	  of	  Nervous	  System	   	   Cerebrum	   • Has	  four	  lobes:	  Frontal,	  Parietal,	  Temporal,	  Occipital	   o These	  do	  not	  correspond	  well	  with	  the	  bones	    Frontal	  lobe	  >	  bone	  	    Parietal	  lobe	  <	  bone	  	    Temporal	  lobe	  –	  part	  is	  in	  the	  middle	  cranial	  fossa	  over	  the	  sphenoid	  bone	   	   Cranial	  nerves	   • All	  but	  olfactory	  and	  optic	  are	  connected	  to	   brain	  stem	   • Olfactory	  to	  cerebral	  hemisphere	   • Optic	  to	  diencephalon	   • Nerves	  are	  of	  motor,	  sensory	  and	  mixed.	   	   	   	   	   	   One	  and	  Two	  ...for	  smell	  and	  sight	   Three,	  four	  and	  six	  ...look	  up	  and	  down	  or	  left	  and	   right	   Five	  to	  chew	  ...and	  feel	  all	  over	  the	  face	   Seven	  to	  smile,	  frown  ...and	  speak	  and	  shrug	   Twelve	  to	  do...	  ...all	  you	  want	  with	  the	  tongue!	   	   Parts	  of	  the	  brain	   • See	  lecture	  27	   • Hind	  brain	  =	  pons	  +	  medulla	  oblongata	   	   Embryology	  of	  the	  brain	   	   Neural	  Tube	  and	  crest	  –	  ectodermal	  in	  origin	  	   • Ectoderm	  thickens	  in	  midline	  =	  neural	  plate	  (during	   wk	  3)	   • Ectodermal	  layer	  pouches	  and	  forms	  a	  longitudinal	  neural	  groove.	   • The	  peaks	  of	  the	  groove	  is	  the	  neural	  crest.	  	   • Crests	  form	  due	  to	  increased	  proliferation	  rate	  	   • The	  crests	  fold	  over	  towards	  each	  other	  and	  form	  the	  neural	  tube.	   o Tube	  closes	  from	  middle	  and	  this	  closure	  migrates	  cranially	  and	   caudally	  simultaneously.	   o Tube	  forms	  brain	  and	  spinal	  cord	   Parts of the Brain Medulla oblongata Myelencephalon Cerebellum PonsMetencephalonRhombencephalon (Hindbrain) Brainstem Midbrain--Mesencephalon (Midbrain) Thalamus etc. Diencephalon Often grouped together as the cerebrum. Cerebral hemispheres TelencephalonProsencephalon (Forebrain) RegionsSecondary vesiclesPrimary vesicles A B C D E • The	  tube	  sinks	  into	  mesoderm,	  followed	  by	  2	  columns	  of	  neural	  crest	  cell	  collections.	   o Crest	  cells	  forms	  the	  dorsal	  root	  ganglion	  and	  other	  ganglia	  of	  PNS	  and	  other	  structures	  in	  the	   body	  (eg	  melanocytes)	  by	  migrating	  to	  the	  appropriate	  area.	   • A	  failure	  of	  the	  neural	  tube	  to	  close	  is	  called	  spina	  bifida	   • Finished	  by	  middle	  of	  wk	  4	   This	  development	  occurs	  at	  the	  same	  time	  as	  the	  vertebral	  column	   	   Primary	  Divisions	  of	  Brain	  (5th	  wk)	   • 3	  divisions	  or	  vesicles:	  Forebrain	  (Prosencephalon),	  Midbrain	  (Mesencephalon),	  Hindbrain	   (Rhombencephalon)	   • Produced	  by	  the	  narrowing/bending	  of	  the	  tube.	   • Mesencephalic	  flexure	  separates	  midbrain	  from	  forebrain	   • The	  pontine	  flexure	  (second	  bend)	  separates	  the	  brain	  from	  brain	  stem	  (this	  is	  actually	  between	  the	   metencephalon	  and	  the	  myelencephalon	  =	  btw	  the	  pons	  and	  the	  medulla	  oblongata)	   • The	  cervical	  flexures	  separates	  the	  brainstem	  from	  the	  spinal	  cord	   	   Secondary	  Divisions	  (7th	  wk)	   • The	  primary	  divisions	  of	  the	  brain	  further	  divide	   • Forebrain:	   o Telencephalon	  –	  “end”	  brain	    Enlarges	  significantly	  and	  laterally	  to	  become	  the	  cerebral	  hemispheres	   o Diencephalon	  –	  “in	  between”	    Forms	  the	  thalamus,	  ventricles	  and	  the	  retina	  of	  the	  eye	  	  (light	  blue	  dot	  on	   image)	   • Mesencephalon	  (mid	  brain)	  =	  no	  divisions	   • Hind	  Brain	   o Metencephalon	  –	  pons	   o Myelencephalon	  –	  medulla	  oblongata	   • Rhombic	  Lip	   o From	  brain	  stem	   o Forms	  cerebellum	  behind	  pons.	   	   Growth	  of	  secondary	  divisions	   • Telencephalon	  expands	  forming	  each	  cerebrum	   • The	  eye	  portion	  of	  the	  telencephalon	  migrates	  anteriorly	   • The	  pons	  grows	  and	  bulges	  forward	   • The	  cerebellum	  develops	  dorsally	  to	  the	  pons	   • The	  mid	  brain	  is	  still	  visible	  but	  will	  soon	  be	  hidden	  by	  the	  growing	   telencephalon	   • Angle	  between	  brainstem	  and	  spinal	  cord	  becomes	  more	  acute	   • As	  the	  neurons	  and	  the	  supporting	  glial	  cells	  proliferate,	  the	  space	   between	  each	  other	  and	  the	  cranial	  cavity	  becomes	  smaller.	  This	   space	  will	  become	  the	  ventricular	  system	  and	  central	  canal	  of	  spinal	   cord	   	   	   Primary Divisions Forebrain (Prosencephalon) Midbrain (Mesencephalon) Hindbrain (Rhombencephalon) The neural tube bends Three divisions (Two ‘flexures’) Remember – it is a tube! F M H D M M.o. T Cerebellum S.c. As neurons and neuroglia grow… …cavities become smaller Ventricular system. as	  cranially	  as	  everything	  is	  in	  the	  cranium.	   • We	  base	  it	  around	  the	  Rostrum	  –	  the	  very	  tip	  of	  the	  corpus	  callosum.	   o It	  is	  the	  closest	  structure	  to	  what	  used	  to	  be	  the	  very	  tip	  of	  the	  neural	  tube	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   	   Growth	  of	  neurons	   	   • 	  The	  neurons	  within	  the	  tube	  wall	  differentiate.	   o Differentiate	  in	  waves	   • Neurons	  bodies	  develop	  first	  and	  remain	  where	  they	  develop	  (associate	  with	  dermo-­‐myotomes	  early	  in	   development)	   • The	  axons	  develop	  and	  then	  migrate	  to	  wherever.	  Eg	  may	  follow	  dermomyotome	  or	  up	  to	  brain	  cortex	   o May	  do	  so	  in	  tracts	   	   Sensory	  Neuron	   • From	  the	  neural	  crest	  cells.	   • Develop	  in	  the	  ganglia	   • Two	  processes	  coming	  out	  of	  nerve	  cell	  body	   	   o Peripheral	  process	  –	  goes	  out	  to	  the	  portion	  of	  the	  body	  it	  is	  sensing	    Dendritic	  in	  direction	  of	  neural	  impulses,	  like	  an	  axon	  in	  the	  speed/functioning	   o Axon/Central	  Process	    Towards	  the	  CNS	   	   Motor	  Neuron	   • Neurons	  in	  the	  cortex	  grow	  down	  the	  spinal	  cord	   • Axons	  grow	  with	  the	  somite/myotome.	   	   Autonomic*	  more	  info?	   • Post	  ganglionic	  fibres	  grow	  outwards	  as	  axons	   • Within	  the	  lateral	  horn	  of	  the	  spinal	  cord	  –	  but	  these	  are	  only	  seen	  in	  the	  thoracic	  and	  upper	  lumbar	   regions	  (SNS)	   	   Functional	  components	  of	  nerves	  	   • Spinal	  cord	  and	  brainstem	  easily	  dividable	  into	  groups	   • Basal	  Lamina	  –	  Motor	  neurons	   • Alar	  Lamina	  –	  Sensory	  neurons	  	   • Sulcus	  limitans	  btw	  basal	  and	  alar	  lamina	   	   	   	   	   	   Classification	   • Sensory	  (Afferent)	   o General	  (GSA	  and	  GVA)	    Somatic	  –	  touch,	  pain,	  temperature,	  joints,	   muscles,	  tendons	    Visceral	  –	  from	  internal	  organs	   o Special	  	  (SSA	  and	  SVA)	    Somatic	  –	  vision,	  hearing	  and	  balance	    Visceral	  –	  Taste	  and	  smell	   • Motor	  (Efferent)	  -­‐	  (GSE	  and	  GVE)	   o Somatic	  	    Skeletal	  muscles	  of	  trunk	  and	  limb	   o Visceral	    Smooth	  and	  cardiac	  muscle	  +	  glands	  (including	  PNS)	   o Branchial	    Muscles	  of	  pharyngeal	  (branchial)	  arches:	  mastication,	  face,	  larynx,	  pharynx	   	   This	  arrangement	  is	  best	  seen	  in	  the	  medulla	  and	  pons	  	   Functional Components Basal Alar GSE SVE GVE GVA SVA GSA SSA This arrangement : best seen in medulla and pons. Somatic motor : most skeletal muscle SVE : “Special” skeletal muscle in head and neck Visceral : smooth muscle / cardiac muscle / glands Visceral sensations – characteristic features. Somatic afferent : all general ‘body sensations’. Special somatic sensations : In brainstem : hearing and equilibrium. “Functional Components” of nerves D V Roof Plate Floor Plate Basal Lamina Alar Lamina Easily recognisable neuron groups in the spinal cord and brainstem Basal Lamina : Motor neurons Alar Lamina : Sensory neurons Sulcus limitans
